turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: The second case is Bank of America versus Miami. The City of Miami in this case brought a suit against two banks under the Fair Housing Act. That statute prohibits among other things racial discrimination in real estate transactions. The city in its complainant claims that the banks engaged in predatory lending practices, that they aimed those practices at African-American and Latino neighborhoods, that they thereby induced defaults in those minority neighborhoods, that that discriminatory conduct led to a disproportionate number of foreclosures and vacancies in those neighborhoods and that their practices thereby impaired the city's efforts to assure racial integration, diminished the city's property tax revenue and increased demand for police, fire and other municipal services all to the detriment of the city's treasury. The question in these cases is whether the Fair Housing Act authorizes the city to bring this kind of lawsuit to recover for its alleged financial injuries. Now, we have here two related questions. First concerns statutory standing, does the Fair Housing Act grant the city a cause of action? We ordinarily say that a plaintiff has a cause of action when its interest “Fall within the zone of interest protected by the law invoked” and we hold that the city's claims of financial injury here do fall within the statutes zone of interest. We have previously held that parties similarly situated to the city can bring suit to recover similar damages. For instance, we held that a village could bring suit for damages arising for a realtor's racial steering practices where those practices caused reduction of property values that injured the village by diminishing its tax base and threatening its ability to provide municipal services. After that decision and others like it, Congress amended the Fair Housing Act and when it did so, it did not significantly change the broad language defining who can bring suit under the statute. So principles of stare decisis respect for previous cases and congressional ratification lead us to conclude that the city does have a cause of action under the Fair Housing Act. The second question is whether the city can seek damages for those two economic injuries that it alleges here; the loss of property tax revenue and the resulting increase in municipal spending. That's a question of causation. The Court of Appeals held that the city's losses were proximately caused by the bank's misconduct because the city's losses were foreseeable. We hold that this statute, this particular statute, the Fair Housing Act, insists upon something more than just foreseeability. Our cases, when discussing common law requirements have referred to the need to show a direct relationship between a claimed injury and claimed misconduct in many actions those cases involve that are common law actions. We believe that here, the Fair Housing Act operates similarly. We do not now define how direct the causal relationship must be except to refer to other cases that say that the nature of the relationship depends upon “The nature of the statutory cause of action” as well as assessment of what is “Administratively possible and convenient.” We leave it for the Court of Appeals on remand to identify the precise boundaries, approximate cause under the Fair Housing Act, and to decide whether the city's damages claims fall within those boundaries. We vacate the judgment of the Court of Appeals for the Eleventh Circuit. We remand the cases for further proceedings consistent with our opinion. Justice Thomas has filed an opinion concurring in part and dissenting in part in which Justices Kennedy and Alito join. Justice Gorsuch took no part in the consideration or decision of these cases.
